questions.question: What is the primary reason for classification of animals?

options.B: To assign systematic position and manage over a million species

questions.explanation: As over a million species of animals have been described, classification helps manage diversity and assign systematic positions to newly described species.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT used as a basis for animal classification?
questions.explanation: Color is not a fundamental basis for classification. Features like body symmetry, coelom, cell arrangement, and digestive/circulatory/reproductive system patterns are used.

questions.question: In sponges, cells are arranged as loose cell aggregates. This represents which level of organisation?
questions.explanation: In sponges (Porifera), cells are arranged as loose cell aggregates, representing cellular level of organisation.

questions.question: In which phylum do cells performing the same function arrange into tissues, representing tissue level of organisation?
questions.explanation: In coelenterates, cells performing the same function are arranged into tissues, hence called tissue level of organisation.

questions.question: Organ level of organisation is first exhibited by members of which phylum?
questions.explanation: Organ level of organisation is exhibited by members of Platyhelminthes and other higher phyla, where tissues are grouped to form organs.

questions.question: Which level of organisation is seen in Annelids, Arthropods, Molluscs, Echinoderms and Chordates?
questions.explanation: In Annelids, Arthropods, Molluscs, Echinoderms and Chordates, organs associate to form functional systems — this is organ system level of organisation.

questions.question: The digestive system in Platyhelminthes has only one opening to the outside. This type of digestive system is called:
questions.explanation: The digestive system in Platyhelminthes has a single opening serving as both mouth and anus, hence called incomplete.

questions.question: A complete digestive system has:
questions.explanation: A complete digestive system has two separate openings: mouth and anus.

questions.question: In an open type circulatory system:

options.B: Blood is pumped out and cells are directly bathed in it
options.C: Blood flows through arteries, veins and capillaries

questions.explanation: In open type circulatory system, blood is pumped out of the heart and cells/tissues are directly bathed in it.

questions.question: In a closed type circulatory system, blood is circulated through:

options.C: A series of vessels of varying diameters

questions.explanation: In closed type circulatory system, blood circulates through vessels of varying diameters — arteries, veins and capillaries.

questions.question: Which group of animals is mostly asymmetrical?
questions.explanation: Sponges are mostly asymmetrical — any plane passing through the centre does not divide them into equal halves.

questions.question: Radial symmetry means:

options.A: Body divided into left and right halves in one plane
options.B: Any plane through central axis divides organism into two identical halves
options.C: No plane divides the body into equal halves

questions.explanation: Radial symmetry means any plane passing through the central axis of the body divides the organism into two identical halves.

questions.question: Which of the following groups exhibit radial symmetry?
questions.explanation: Coelenterates, ctenophores and echinoderms exhibit radial symmetry.

questions.question: Bilateral symmetry means the body can be divided into identical halves in:

options.C: Only one plane — left and right

questions.explanation: Bilateral symmetry means the body can be divided into identical left and right halves in only one plane.

questions.question: Which of the following animals exhibit bilateral symmetry?
questions.explanation: Annelids like earthworm exhibit bilateral symmetry. Hydra and Aurelia are coelenterates with radial symmetry. Starfish (echinoderm) shows radial symmetry in adults.

questions.question: Diploblastic animals are those in which cells are arranged in how many embryonic layers?
questions.explanation: Diploblastic animals have cells arranged in two embryonic layers — external ectoderm and internal endoderm.

questions.question: Which undifferentiated layer is present between ectoderm and endoderm in diploblastic animals?
questions.explanation: In diploblastic animals (like coelenterates), an undifferentiated layer called mesoglea is present between ectoderm and endoderm.

questions.question: Coelenterates are an example of which type of organisation?
questions.explanation: Coelenterates are diploblastic animals with ectoderm, mesoglea and endoderm.

questions.question: Animals with three embryonic layers (ectoderm, mesoderm, endoderm) are called:
questions.explanation: Animals with a third germinal layer — mesoderm — between ectoderm and endoderm are called triploblastic animals.

questions.question: Triploblastic condition is seen from which phylum to which phylum?
questions.explanation: Triploblastic condition is seen from Platyhelminthes to Chordates.

questions.question: The body cavity lined by mesoderm is called:
questions.explanation: Coelom is the body cavity that is lined by mesoderm.

questions.question: Animals possessing a true coelom (lined by mesoderm) are called:
questions.explanation: Animals possessing a coelom lined by mesoderm are called coelomates.

questions.question: Which of the following is a coelomate phylum?
questions.explanation: Annelida is a coelomate phylum. Platyhelminthes are acoelomate, Aschelminthes are pseudocoelomate.

questions.question: In pseudocoelomates, the mesoderm is present as:

options.A: A continuous lining of the body cavity
options.B: Scattered pouches between ectoderm and endoderm
options.C: A thick layer under the skin

questions.explanation: In pseudocoelomates, the mesoderm is present as scattered pouches in between ectoderm and endoderm, and the body cavity is not lined by mesoderm.

questions.question: Aschelminthes (roundworms) are examples of:
questions.explanation: Aschelminthes are pseudocoelomates — their body cavity is not lined by mesoderm.

questions.question: Animals in which the body cavity is completely absent are called:
questions.explanation: Acoelomates are animals in which the body cavity is absent, e.g., Platyhelminthes.

questions.question: Platyhelminthes are classified as:
questions.explanation: Platyhelminthes (flatworms) are acoelomates — they lack a body cavity.

questions.question: Segmentation in which organs are serially repeated is called:
questions.explanation: Metameric segmentation (metamerism) is the pattern where the body is divided into segments with serial repetition of at least some organs, as seen in earthworm.

questions.question: Which animal is the classic example of metameric segmentation?
questions.explanation: Earthworm is the classic example of metameric segmentation, where the body is externally and internally divided into segments.

questions.question: Notochord is a structure derived from:
questions.explanation: Notochord is a mesodermally derived rod-like structure formed on the dorsal side during embryonic development.

questions.question: Animals that possess a notochord are called:
questions.explanation: Animals with notochord are called chordates and those without are called non-chordates.

questions.question: Which phyla are classified as non-chordates?
questions.explanation: Non-chordates include animals from Porifera to Echinoderms — animals that do not form the notochord.

questions.question: Members of Phylum Porifera are commonly known as:
questions.explanation: Members of Phylum Porifera are commonly known as sponges.

questions.question: Sponges are generally:
questions.explanation: Sponges are generally marine and mostly asymmetrical animals.

questions.question: Sponges are primitive multicellular animals exhibiting which level of organisation?
questions.explanation: Sponges are primitive multicellular animals with cellular level of organisation.

questions.question: Water enters the sponge body through:
questions.explanation: Water enters through minute pores called ostia in the body wall of sponges.

questions.question: In sponges, water enters the body wall through ostia and enters into a central cavity called:
questions.explanation: Water enters through ostia into the central cavity called spongocoel.

questions.question: Water exits the sponge body through:
questions.explanation: Water goes out through the osculum after entering through ostia into the spongocoel.

questions.question: The water transport system in sponges is useful for:

options.B: Food gathering, respiratory exchange and removal of waste

questions.explanation: The water transport system (canal system) in sponges helps in food gathering, respiratory exchange and removal of waste.

questions.question: Which cells line the spongocoel and canals in sponges?
questions.explanation: Choanocytes or collar cells line the spongocoel and the canals in sponges.

questions.question: Digestion in sponges is:
questions.explanation: Digestion in sponges is intracellular.

questions.question: The skeleton of sponges is made up of:
questions.explanation: The body of sponges is supported by a skeleton made up of spicules or spongin fibres.

questions.question: Sponges are hermaphrodite, which means:

options.B: Eggs and sperms are produced by the same individual

questions.explanation: Hermaphrodite means sexes are not separate — eggs and sperms are produced by the same individual. Sponges are hermaphrodite.

questions.question: Sponges reproduce asexually by:
questions.explanation: Sponges reproduce asexually by fragmentation and sexually by formation of gametes.

questions.question: Fertilisation in sponges is:
questions.explanation: Fertilisation in sponges is internal.

questions.question: Development in sponges is:

options.B: Indirect with a larval stage morphologically distinct from adult
options.C: Indirect with a larval stage identical to adult

questions.explanation: Development in sponges is indirect — there is a larval stage that is morphologically distinct from the adult.

questions.question: Which of the following is a freshwater sponge?
questions.explanation: Spongilla is a freshwater sponge. Sycon (Scypha) and Euspongia (bath sponge) are marine sponges.

questions.question: Euspongia is commonly known as:
questions.explanation: Euspongia is commonly known as bath sponge.

questions.question: Members of Phylum Coelenterata are:

options.B: Aquatic, mostly marine, sessile or free-swimming, radially symmetrical

questions.explanation: Coelenterates are aquatic, mostly marine, sessile or free-swimming, radially symmetrical animals.

questions.question: The name 'Cnidaria' is derived from the Greek word for:
questions.explanation: The name Cnidaria is derived from cnidoblasts or cnidocytes (stinging cells/capsules).

questions.question: Cnidoblasts (cnidocytes) are present on:

options.B: The tentacles and body of cnidarians

questions.explanation: Cnidoblasts or cnidocytes (which contain stinging capsules or nematocysts) are present on the tentacles and the body of cnidarians.

questions.question: Cnidoblasts are used for:

options.B: Anchorage, defense and capture of prey

questions.explanation: Cnidoblasts are used for anchorage, defense and for the capture of prey.

questions.question: Stinging capsules present inside cnidoblasts are called:
questions.explanation: Cnidoblasts contain stinging capsules called nematocysts.

questions.question: Cnidarians exhibit which level of organisation?
questions.explanation: Cnidarians exhibit tissue level of organisation.

questions.question: Cnidarians are:
questions.explanation: Cnidarians are diploblastic organisms.

questions.question: The central gastro-vascular cavity in cnidarians has:

options.C: A single opening — mouth on hypostome

questions.explanation: Cnidarians have a central gastro-vascular cavity with a single opening — mouth on hypostome.

questions.question: Digestion in cnidarians is:
questions.explanation: Digestion in cnidarians is both extracellular and intracellular.

questions.question: Which cnidarians have a skeleton composed of calcium carbonate?
questions.explanation: Some cnidarians, e.g., corals, have a skeleton composed of calcium carbonate.

questions.question: The two basic body forms of cnidarians are:
questions.explanation: Cnidarians exhibit two basic body forms — polyp and medusa.

questions.question: Polyp form in cnidarians is:
questions.explanation: Polyp is a sessile and cylindrical form, like Hydra and Adamsia.

questions.question: Medusa form in cnidarians is:
questions.explanation: Medusa is umbrella-shaped and free-swimming, like Aurelia (jellyfish).

questions.question: Metagenesis refers to:

options.B: Alternation of generations between polyp and medusa

questions.explanation: Metagenesis is alternation of generations — polyps produce medusae asexually and medusae form polyps sexually.

questions.question: Which cnidarian exhibits metagenesis (both polyp and medusa forms)?
questions.explanation: Obelia exhibits metagenesis — it exists in both polyp and medusa forms.

questions.question: Physalia is commonly known as:
questions.explanation: Physalia is commonly known as Portuguese man-of-war.

questions.question: Adamsia is commonly known as:
questions.explanation: Adamsia is commonly known as sea anemone.

questions.question: Meandrina is commonly known as:
questions.explanation: Meandrina is commonly known as brain coral.

questions.question: Ctenophores are commonly known as:
questions.explanation: Ctenophores are commonly known as sea walnuts or comb jellies.

questions.question: Ctenophores are:
questions.explanation: Ctenophores are exclusively marine organisms.

questions.question: Which of the following correctly describes ctenophores?

options.B: Radially symmetrical, diploblastic, marine with tissue level of organisation

questions.explanation: Ctenophores are exclusively marine, radially symmetrical, diploblastic organisms with tissue level of organisation.

questions.question: Comb plates in ctenophores help in:
questions.explanation: The body of ctenophores bears eight external rows of ciliated comb plates which help in locomotion.

questions.question: How many external rows of ciliated comb plates do ctenophores bear?
questions.explanation: Ctenophores bear eight external rows of ciliated comb plates for locomotion.

questions.question: Bioluminescence is well-marked in which phylum?
questions.explanation: Bioluminescence (property of living organisms to emit light) is well-marked in ctenophores.

questions.question: Reproduction in ctenophores occurs by:
questions.explanation: Reproduction in ctenophores takes place only by sexual means.

questions.question: Fertilisation in ctenophores is:
questions.explanation: In ctenophores, fertilisation is external with indirect development.

questions.question: Pleurobrachia belongs to which phylum?
questions.explanation: Pleurobrachia is an example of Phylum Ctenophora.

questions.question: Members of Platyhelminthes are called flatworms because they have:
questions.explanation: Platyhelminthes have dorso-ventrally flattened body, hence called flatworms.

questions.question: Platyhelminthes are mostly:

options.B: Endoparasites found in animals including humans

questions.explanation: Platyhelminthes are mostly endoparasites found in animals including human beings.

questions.question: Which of the following correctly describes Platyhelminthes?

options.B: Bilaterally symmetrical, triploblastic, acoelomate with organ level of organisation

questions.explanation: Flatworms are bilaterally symmetrical, triploblastic and acoelomate animals with organ level of organisation.

questions.question: Hooks and suckers in Platyhelminthes are present in:
questions.explanation: Hooks and suckers are present in the parasitic forms of Platyhelminthes.

questions.question: Some Platyhelminthes absorb nutrients from the host:
questions.explanation: Some platyhelminthes absorb nutrients from the host directly through their body surface.

questions.question: Flame cells in Platyhelminthes help in:
questions.explanation: Specialised cells called flame cells help in osmoregulation and excretion in Platyhelminthes.

questions.question: Which member of Platyhelminthes possesses high regeneration capacity?
questions.explanation: Some members like Planaria possess high regeneration capacity.

questions.question: Taenia is commonly known as:
questions.explanation: Taenia is commonly known as tapeworm and belongs to Phylum Platyhelminthes.

questions.question: Fasciola is commonly known as:
questions.explanation: Fasciola is commonly known as liver fluke and belongs to Phylum Platyhelminthes.

questions.question: Fertilisation in Platyhelminthes is:
questions.explanation: In Platyhelminthes, fertilisation is internal and development is through many larval stages.

questions.question: Are the sexes separate in Platyhelminthes?
questions.explanation: Sexes are not separate in Platyhelminthes — they are hermaphrodite (monoecious).

questions.question: Aschelminthes are commonly known as:
questions.explanation: The body of Aschelminthes is circular in cross-section, hence they are called roundworms.

questions.question: Aschelminthes may be found in which of the following habitats?

options.C: Freeliving (aquatic and terrestrial) or parasitic in plants and animals

questions.explanation: Aschelminthes may be freeliving — aquatic and terrestrial — or parasitic in plants and animals.

questions.question: Which of the following correctly describes Aschelminthes?
questions.explanation: Aschelminthes are bilaterally symmetrical, triploblastic and pseudocoelomate animals.

questions.question: The alimentary canal of Aschelminthes is:

options.C: Complete with a well-developed muscular pharynx

questions.explanation: The alimentary canal in Aschelminthes is complete with a well-developed muscular pharynx.

questions.question: In Aschelminthes, body wastes are removed from the body cavity through the excretory pore via an:
questions.explanation: An excretory tube removes body wastes from the body cavity through the excretory pore in Aschelminthes.

questions.question: In Aschelminthes, sexes are:
questions.explanation: Sexes are separate (dioecious) in Aschelminthes — males and females are distinct.

questions.question: In Aschelminthes, which sex is often longer?
questions.explanation: In Aschelminthes, females are often longer than males.

questions.question: Ascaris belongs to which phylum?
questions.explanation: Ascaris (roundworm) belongs to Phylum Aschelminthes.

questions.question: Wuchereria causes which disease?
questions.explanation: Wuchereria (filaria worm) belongs to Aschelminthes and causes filariasis.

questions.question: Ancylostoma is commonly known as:
questions.explanation: Ancylostoma is commonly known as hookworm and belongs to Phylum Aschelminthes.

questions.question: Which of the following phyla shows organ system level, bilateral symmetry, triploblastic, metameric segmentation and coelomate?
questions.explanation: Annelida exhibits organ system level of body organisation, bilateral symmetry, triploblastic, metamerically segmented and coelomate characteristics.

questions.question: The name Annelida comes from the Latin word 'annulus' which means:
questions.explanation: The phylum name Annelida comes from Latin 'annulus' meaning 'little ring', referring to the ring-like segments.

questions.question: Which structures help aquatic annelids like Nereis in swimming?
questions.explanation: Aquatic annelids like Nereis possess lateral appendages called parapodia which help in swimming.

questions.question: The circulatory system in Annelida is:
questions.explanation: A closed circulatory system is present in Annelida.

questions.question: Which organs help in osmoregulation and excretion in Annelida?
questions.explanation: Nephridia (singular: nephridium) help in osmoregulation and excretion in Annelida.

questions.question: The neural system of Annelida consists of:

options.B: Paired ganglia connected by lateral nerves to a double ventral nerve cord

questions.explanation: Neural system of Annelida consists of paired ganglia connected by lateral nerves to a double ventral nerve cord.

questions.question: Which statement about sexuality in Annelids is CORRECT?

options.C: Nereis is dioecious, but earthworms and leeches are monoecious

questions.explanation: Nereis (aquatic annelid) is dioecious, but earthworms and leeches are monoecious (hermaphrodite).

questions.question: Pheretima is the scientific name for:
questions.explanation: Pheretima is the scientific name for earthworm, which belongs to Phylum Annelida.

questions.question: Hirudinaria is commonly known as:
questions.explanation: Hirudinaria is commonly known as blood sucking leech and belongs to Phylum Annelida.

questions.question: Which phylum is the largest phylum of Animalia?
questions.explanation: Arthropoda is the largest phylum of Animalia, including insects. Over two-thirds of all named species on earth are arthropods.

questions.question: What fraction of all named species on Earth are arthropods?
questions.explanation: Over two-thirds of all named species on Earth are arthropods.

questions.question: The body of arthropods is covered by:
questions.explanation: The body of arthropods is covered by a chitinous exoskeleton.

questions.question: The body of arthropods consists of:

options.D: Head, muscular foot and visceral hump

questions.explanation: The body of arthropods consists of head, thorax and abdomen.

questions.question: The name Arthropoda is derived from Greek words meaning:
questions.explanation: Arthropoda derives from Greek: arthros (joint) + poda (appendages) — arthropods have jointed appendages.

questions.question: Respiratory organs in Arthropods can be:

options.C: Gills, book gills, book lungs or tracheal system

questions.explanation: Respiratory organs in arthropods can be gills, book gills, book lungs or tracheal system.

questions.question: The circulatory system in Arthropoda is:
questions.explanation: Circulatory system in Arthropoda is of open type.

questions.question: Sensory organs in Arthropods include:
questions.explanation: Sensory organs in arthropods include antennae, eyes (compound and simple), and statocysts (balancing organs).

questions.question: Excretion in Arthropods takes place through:
questions.explanation: Excretion in arthropods takes place through malpighian tubules.

questions.question: Arthropods are mostly:
questions.explanation: Arthropods are mostly dioecious. Fertilisation is usually internal. They are mostly oviparous.

questions.question: Apis is the scientific name for:
questions.explanation: Apis is the scientific name for honey bee, an economically important arthropod.

questions.question: Bombyx is the scientific name for:
questions.explanation: Bombyx is the scientific name for silkworm, an economically important arthropod.

questions.question: Which arthropod is called the 'Living fossil'?
questions.explanation: Limulus (King crab) is called the living fossil among arthropods.

questions.question: Locusta is classified as a:
questions.explanation: Locusta (Locust) is classified as a gregarious pest.

questions.question: Which mosquitoes are classified as vectors in Arthropoda?
questions.explanation: Anopheles, Culex and Aedes (mosquitoes) are classified as vectors in arthropods.

questions.question: Phylum Mollusca is the:
questions.explanation: Mollusca is the second largest animal phylum (Arthropoda is the largest).

questions.question: Molluscs can be found in which habitat?

options.C: Terrestrial or aquatic (marine or fresh water)

questions.explanation: Molluscs are terrestrial or aquatic (marine or fresh water) animals.

questions.question: Which of the following correctly describes Mollusca?
questions.explanation: Molluscs are bilaterally symmetrical, triploblastic and coelomate animals.

questions.question: The body of molluscs is covered by:
questions.explanation: The body of molluscs is covered by a calcareous shell.

questions.question: Molluscs have an unsegmented body with distinct:

options.B: Head, muscular foot and visceral hump

questions.explanation: Molluscs have an unsegmented body with a distinct head, muscular foot and visceral hump.

questions.question: The mantle in Mollusca is:

options.B: A soft and spongy layer of skin over the visceral hump

questions.explanation: A soft and spongy layer of skin forms the mantle over the visceral hump in Mollusca.

questions.question: The mantle cavity in Mollusca contains:

options.B: Feather-like gills for respiration and excretion

questions.explanation: The space between the visceral hump and mantle is the mantle cavity, which contains feather-like gills having respiratory and excretory functions.

questions.question: The feeding organ in Mollusca called radula is:

options.A: A file-like rasping organ in the mouth

questions.explanation: The mouth of molluscs contains a file-like rasping organ for feeding, called radula.

questions.question: Are molluscs dioecious or monoecious?
questions.explanation: Molluscs are usually dioecious and oviparous with indirect development.

questions.question: Pinctada is the scientific name for:
questions.explanation: Pinctada is the scientific name for pearl oyster, belonging to Phylum Mollusca.

questions.question: Sepia belongs to Phylum Mollusca. It is commonly known as:
questions.explanation: Sepia is commonly known as cuttlefish.

questions.question: Octopus is also known as:
questions.explanation: Octopus is commonly known as devil fish.

questions.question: All members of Phylum Echinodermata are:
questions.explanation: All echinoderms are marine animals.

questions.question: The name Echinodermata means:
questions.explanation: Echinodermata means 'spiny bodied' — these animals have an endoskeleton of calcareous ossicles.

questions.question: The endoskeleton of echinoderms is made of:
questions.explanation: Echinoderms have an endoskeleton of calcareous ossicles.

questions.question: The symmetry of adult echinoderms vs their larvae is:
questions.explanation: Adult echinoderms are radially symmetrical but larvae are bilaterally symmetrical.

questions.question: The most distinctive feature of echinoderms is the presence of:
questions.explanation: The most distinctive feature of echinoderms is the presence of the water vascular system.

questions.question: The water vascular system in echinoderms helps in:

options.B: Locomotion, capture and transport of food and respiration

questions.explanation: The water vascular system in echinoderms helps in locomotion, capture and transport of food and respiration.

questions.question: The excretory system in echinoderms is:
questions.explanation: An excretory system is absent in echinoderms.

questions.question: In echinoderms, the mouth is on which side?
questions.explanation: In echinoderms, the digestive system is complete with mouth on the lower (ventral) side and anus on the upper (dorsal) side.

questions.question: Fertilisation in echinoderms is usually:
questions.explanation: In echinoderms, fertilisation is usually external.

questions.question: Development in echinoderms is:
questions.explanation: Development in echinoderms is indirect with free-swimming larva.

questions.question: Asterias is the scientific name for:
questions.explanation: Asterias is the scientific name for starfish, belonging to Phylum Echinodermata.

questions.question: Echinus is the scientific name for:
questions.explanation: Echinus is the scientific name for sea urchin.

questions.question: Antedon is the scientific name for:
questions.explanation: Antedon is the scientific name for sea lily, belonging to Phylum Echinodermata.

questions.question: Ophiura is the scientific name for:
questions.explanation: Ophiura is the scientific name for brittle star.

questions.question: Hemichordata was earlier considered as a sub-phylum under which phylum?
questions.explanation: Hemichordata was earlier considered as a sub-phylum under phylum Chordata but is now placed as a separate phylum under non-chordata.

questions.question: Hemichordates have a rudimentary structure in the collar region called:
questions.explanation: Hemichordates have a rudimentary structure in the collar region called stomochord, similar to notochord.

questions.question: The body of hemichordates is composed of:

options.B: Head, muscular foot and visceral hump
options.C: Anterior proboscis, a collar and a long trunk

questions.explanation: The body of hemichordates is cylindrical and composed of an anterior proboscis, a collar and a long trunk.

questions.question: Respiration in Hemichordata takes place through:
questions.explanation: Respiration in hemichordates takes place through gills.

questions.question: The excretory organ in Hemichordata is:
questions.explanation: The excretory organ in hemichordates is the proboscis gland.

questions.question: The circulatory system in Hemichordata is:
questions.explanation: The circulatory system in hemichordates is of open type.

questions.question: Balanoglossus belongs to which phylum?
questions.explanation: Balanoglossus belongs to Phylum Hemichordata.

questions.question: Animals belonging to Phylum Chordata are fundamentally characterised by the presence of:

options.B: Notochord, dorsal hollow nerve cord and paired pharyngeal gill slits

questions.explanation: Chordates are fundamentally characterised by a notochord, dorsal hollow nerve cord and paired pharyngeal gill slits.

questions.question: Chordates possess which type of circulatory system?
questions.explanation: Chordates possess a closed circulatory system.

questions.question: In chordates, the central nervous system is:
questions.explanation: In chordates, the central nervous system is dorsal, hollow and single. In non-chordates it is ventral, solid and double.

questions.question: In non-chordates, the heart (if present) is:
questions.explanation: In non-chordates, the heart is dorsal (if present), whereas in chordates, the heart is ventral.

questions.question: A post-anal tail is present in which group?
questions.explanation: A post-anal part (tail) is present in chordates but absent in non-chordates.

questions.question: Phylum Chordata is divided into how many subphyla?
questions.explanation: Phylum Chordata is divided into three subphyla: Urochordata (Tunicata), Cephalochordata and Vertebrata.

questions.question: Subphyla Urochordata and Cephalochordata together are referred to as:
questions.explanation: Subphyla Urochordata and Cephalochordata are often referred to as protochordates and are exclusively marine.

questions.question: In Urochordata, the notochord is present:

options.C: From head to tail throughout life

questions.explanation: In Urochordata, notochord is present only in the larval tail.

questions.question: In Cephalochordata, the notochord:

options.A: Is present only in larval stage
options.B: Extends from head to tail region and is persistent throughout life

questions.explanation: In Cephalochordata, the notochord extends from head to tail region and is persistent throughout their life.

questions.question: Branchiostoma (Amphioxus or Lancelet) belongs to which subphylum?
questions.explanation: Branchiostoma (Amphioxus or Lancelet) belongs to subphylum Cephalochordata.

questions.question: Ascidia and Salpa belong to which subphylum?
questions.explanation: Ascidia, Salpa and Doliolum are examples of Urochordata.

questions.question: In members of subphylum Vertebrata, the notochord during adult stage is replaced by:
questions.explanation: In vertebrates, the notochord is replaced by a cartilaginous or bony vertebral column in the adult.

questions.question: Which statement is CORRECT?

options.B: All vertebrates are chordates but all chordates are not vertebrates

questions.explanation: All vertebrates are chordates (they possess notochord in embryonic stage), but all chordates are not vertebrates (protochordates are chordates but not vertebrates).

questions.question: Vertebrates additionally have which features besides basic chordate characters?

options.B: Ventral muscular heart with 2-4 chambers, kidneys, paired appendages

questions.explanation: Vertebrates additionally have a ventral muscular heart with two, three or four chambers, kidneys for excretion and osmoregulation, and paired appendages which may be fins or limbs.

questions.question: The class Cyclostomata belongs to which division of Vertebrata?
questions.explanation: Class Cyclostomata belongs to Division Agnatha (lacks jaw) of Vertebrata.

questions.question: All living members of Class Cyclostomata are:
questions.explanation: All living members of class Cyclostomata are ectoparasites on some fishes.

questions.question: How many pairs of gill slits do cyclostomes have for respiration?
questions.explanation: Cyclostomes have an elongated body bearing 6-15 pairs of gill slits for respiration.

questions.question: Cyclostomes have a sucking circular mouth without:
questions.explanation: Cyclostomes have a sucking and circular mouth without jaws.

questions.question: The body of cyclostomes is devoid of:
questions.explanation: The body of cyclostomes is devoid of scales and paired fins.

questions.question: The cranium and vertebral column in cyclostomes are:
questions.explanation: Cranium and vertebral column in cyclostomes are cartilaginous.

questions.question: Cyclostomes are marine but migrate to freshwater for:
questions.explanation: Cyclostomes are marine but migrate to freshwater for spawning. After spawning, within a few days, they die.

questions.question: After metamorphosis, cyclostome larvae return to:
questions.explanation: Their larvae, after metamorphosis, return to the ocean.

questions.question: Petromyzon is commonly known as:
questions.explanation: Petromyzon is commonly known as lamprey and belongs to Class Cyclostomata.

questions.question: Myxine is commonly known as:
questions.explanation: Myxine is commonly known as hagfish and belongs to Class Cyclostomata.

questions.question: Class Chondrichthyes animals have which type of endoskeleton?
questions.explanation: Chondrichthyes are marine animals with cartilaginous endoskeleton.

questions.question: In Chondrichthyes, the mouth is located:
questions.explanation: In Chondrichthyes (cartilaginous fishes), the mouth is located ventrally.

questions.question: In Chondrichthyes, gill slits are:
questions.explanation: In Chondrichthyes, gill slits are separate and without operculum (gill cover).

questions.question: The skin of Chondrichthyes is tough and contains:
questions.explanation: The skin of Chondrichthyes is tough and contains minute placoid scales.

questions.question: Teeth in Chondrichthyes are modified:
questions.explanation: Teeth in Chondrichthyes are modified placoid scales which are backwardly directed.

questions.question: Why do Chondrichthyes have to swim constantly?

options.B: Due to the absence of air bladder — to avoid sinking

questions.explanation: Due to the absence of air bladder, Chondrichthyes have to swim constantly to avoid sinking.

questions.question: The heart of Chondrichthyes has:
questions.explanation: The heart in Chondrichthyes is two-chambered with one auricle and one ventricle.

questions.question: Which Chondrichthyes has electric organs?
questions.explanation: Torpedo has electric organs.

questions.question: Which Chondrichthyes possesses poison sting?
questions.explanation: Trygon (sting ray) possesses poison sting.

questions.question: Cold-blooded animals are scientifically termed as:
questions.explanation: Cold-blooded animals (those that cannot regulate body temperature) are called poikilothermous.

questions.question: In male Chondrichthyes, pelvic fins bear:
questions.explanation: In male Chondrichthyes, pelvic fins bear claspers.

questions.question: Many Chondrichthyes are:
questions.explanation: Chondrichthyes have internal fertilisation and many of them are viviparous.

questions.question: Scoliodon is commonly known as:
questions.explanation: Scoliodon is commonly known as dog fish and belongs to Class Chondrichthyes.

questions.question: Carcharodon is commonly known as:
questions.explanation: Carcharodon is commonly known as the great white shark.

questions.question: Class Osteichthyes includes fishes with:
questions.explanation: Osteichthyes includes both marine and freshwater fishes with bony endoskeleton.

questions.question: In Osteichthyes, gills are covered by:
questions.explanation: In Osteichthyes, four pairs of gills are covered by an operculum on each side.

questions.question: The skin of Osteichthyes is covered with:
questions.explanation: The skin of Osteichthyes is covered with cycloid/ctenoid scales.

questions.question: Air bladder in bony fishes regulates:
questions.explanation: Air bladder is present in Osteichthyes and regulates buoyancy.

questions.question: Fertilisation in Osteichthyes is usually:
questions.explanation: In Osteichthyes, fertilisation is usually external.

questions.question: Exocoetus is commonly known as:
questions.explanation: Exocoetus is commonly known as flying fish and is a marine bony fish.

questions.question: Hippocampus is commonly known as:
questions.explanation: Hippocampus is commonly known as sea horse, a marine bony fish.

questions.question: Labeo (Rohu) belongs to which class?
questions.explanation: Labeo (Rohu) is a freshwater bony fish belonging to Class Osteichthyes.

questions.question: Betta (fighting fish) is an example of which type of fish?
questions.explanation: Betta (fighting fish) is an aquarium fish belonging to Class Osteichthyes.

questions.question: Amphibians can live in:
questions.explanation: Amphibians (Gr. Amphi = dual, bios = life) can live in aquatic as well as terrestrial habitats.

questions.question: The amphibian skin is:
questions.explanation: The amphibian skin is moist (without scales).

questions.question: In Amphibia, the alimentary canal, urinary and reproductive tracts open into a common chamber called:
questions.explanation: In amphibians, the alimentary canal, urinary and reproductive tracts open into a common chamber called cloaca which opens to the exterior.

questions.question: Respiration in Amphibia is by:
questions.explanation: Respiration in Amphibia is by gills, lungs and through skin.

questions.question: The heart in Amphibia has:
questions.explanation: Amphibians have a three-chambered heart — two auricles and one ventricle.

questions.question: Are amphibians warm-blooded or cold-blooded?
questions.explanation: Amphibians are cold-blooded (poikilothermous) animals.

questions.question: Fertilisation in Amphibia is:
questions.explanation: Fertilisation in Amphibia is external.

questions.question: Development in Amphibia is:
questions.explanation: Amphibians are oviparous and development is indirect.

questions.question: Bufo is the scientific name for:
questions.explanation: Bufo is the scientific name for toad, belonging to Class Amphibia.

questions.question: Rana is the scientific name for:
questions.explanation: Rana is the scientific name for frog, belonging to Class Amphibia.

questions.question: Ichthyophis is an example of:
questions.explanation: Ichthyophis is an example of limbless amphibia, belonging to Class Amphibia.

questions.question: The class name Reptilia refers to their:

options.B: Creeping or crawling mode of locomotion

questions.explanation: The class name Reptilia comes from Latin 'repere or reptum' meaning to creep or crawl — referring to their mode of locomotion.

questions.question: The body of reptiles is covered by:

options.C: Dry and cornified skin, epidermal scales or scutes

questions.explanation: Reptile body is covered by dry and cornified skin, epidermal scales or scutes.

questions.question: Reptiles do NOT have:
questions.explanation: Reptiles do not have external ear openings. Tympanum represents the ear in reptiles.

questions.question: The heart in most reptiles is usually:
questions.explanation: Heart is usually three-chambered in reptiles, but four-chambered in crocodiles.

questions.question: Which reptile has a four-chambered heart?
questions.explanation: Among reptiles, crocodiles have a four-chambered heart.

questions.question: Snakes and lizards shed their scales as:
questions.explanation: Snakes and lizards shed their scales as skin cast.

questions.question: Chelone is the scientific name for:
questions.explanation: Chelone is the scientific name for turtle, belonging to Class Reptilia.

questions.question: Testudo is the scientific name for:
questions.explanation: Testudo is the scientific name for tortoise, belonging to Class Reptilia.

questions.question: Naja is the scientific name for:
questions.explanation: Naja is the scientific name for cobra, a poisonous snake belonging to Class Reptilia.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a reptile?
questions.explanation: Salamander (Salamandra) is an amphibian, not a reptile.

questions.question: The most characteristic feature of Class Aves (birds) is the presence of:
questions.explanation: The characteristic feature of Aves is the presence of feathers. Most birds can fly, but some are flightless.

questions.question: An example of a flightless bird is:
questions.explanation: Ostrich (Struthio) is an example of a flightless bird.

questions.question: In birds (Aves), the forelimbs are modified into:
questions.explanation: In Aves, the forelimbs are modified into wings.

questions.question: The endoskeleton in birds is fully ossified and the long bones are hollow with:
questions.explanation: In birds, the endoskeleton is fully ossified (bony) and long bones are hollow with air cavities (pneumatic).

questions.question: The digestive tract of birds has additional chambers called:
questions.explanation: The digestive tract of birds has additional chambers — the crop and gizzard.

questions.question: The heart in birds is:
questions.explanation: Birds have a completely four-chambered heart.

questions.question: Warm-blooded animals that can maintain a constant body temperature are called:
questions.explanation: Warm-blooded animals that can maintain a constant body temperature are called homoiothermous.

questions.question: Respiration in birds is by lungs, supplemented by:
questions.explanation: Respiration in birds is by lungs, and air sacs connected to lungs supplement respiration.

questions.question: Fertilisation in birds is:
questions.explanation: In Aves, fertilisation is internal.

questions.question: Birds are:
questions.explanation: Birds are oviparous and development is direct.

questions.question: Corvus is the scientific name for:
questions.explanation: Corvus is the scientific name for crow, belonging to Class Aves.

questions.question: Pavo is the scientific name for:
questions.explanation: Pavo is the scientific name for peacock, belonging to Class Aves.

questions.question: Aptenodytes is the scientific name for:
questions.explanation: Aptenodytes is the scientific name for penguin, a flightless bird of Class Aves.

questions.question: Neophron is the scientific name for:
questions.explanation: Neophron is the scientific name for vulture, belonging to Class Aves.

questions.question: The most unique mammalian characteristic is the presence of:
questions.explanation: The most unique mammalian characteristic is the presence of milk producing glands (mammary glands) by which young ones are nourished.

questions.question: The skin of mammals is unique in possessing:
questions.explanation: The skin of mammals is unique in possessing hair.

questions.question: External ears or pinnae are present in:
questions.explanation: External ears or pinnae are present in mammals.

questions.question: Mammals are:
questions.explanation: Mammals are homoiothermous (warm-blooded) — they can maintain constant body temperature.

questions.question: Most mammals are:
questions.explanation: Mammals are viviparous with few exceptions (like Platypus which is oviparous).

questions.question: Ornithorhynchus (Platypus) is an oviparous mammal. True or False?

options.D: False — Platypus is not a mammal

questions.explanation: Ornithorhynchus (Platypus) is an oviparous mammal — an exception to the general viviparous nature of mammals.

questions.question: Macropus is the scientific name for:
questions.explanation: Macropus is the scientific name for kangaroo, a viviparous mammal.

questions.question: Pteropus is commonly known as:
questions.explanation: Pteropus is commonly known as flying fox (a type of bat), belonging to Class Mammalia.

questions.question: Balaenoptera is the scientific name for:
questions.explanation: Balaenoptera is the scientific name for blue whale.

questions.question: Panthera tigris is the scientific name for:
questions.explanation: Panthera tigris is the scientific name for tiger.

questions.question: Mammals have how many pairs of limbs typically?
questions.explanation: Mammals have two pairs of limbs, adapted for walking, running, climbing, burrowing, swimming or flying.

questions.question: In mammals, different types of teeth are present in the jaw. This condition is called:
questions.explanation: Mammals are heterodont — different types of teeth (incisors, canines, premolars, molars) are present in the jaw.

questions.question: The heart in mammals is:
questions.explanation: The heart in mammals is four-chambered.

questions.question: Respiration in mammals is by:
questions.explanation: Respiration in mammals is by lungs.

questions.question: Development in mammals is:
questions.explanation: Development in mammals is direct.

questions.question: Which of the following is the correct match of animal and its common name?
questions.explanation: Loligo is the scientific name for squid. Pila=Apple snail, Aplysia=Sea hare, Dentalium=Tusk shell.

questions.question: Cucumaria is the scientific name for:
questions.explanation: Cucumaria is the scientific name for sea cucumber, belonging to Phylum Echinodermata.

questions.question: Which phylum has the characteristic feature of body with pores and canals in walls?
questions.explanation: Body with pores and canals in walls is the distinctive feature of Porifera (sponges).

questions.question: Cnidoblasts present is the distinctive feature of which phylum?
questions.explanation: Cnidoblasts present is the distinctive feature of Phylum Coelenterata (Cnidaria).

questions.question: Comb plates for locomotion is the distinctive feature of:
questions.explanation: Comb plates for locomotion is the distinctive feature of Phylum Ctenophora.

questions.question: Flat body with suckers is the distinctive feature of:
questions.explanation: Flat body with suckers is the distinctive feature of Platyhelminthes.

questions.question: Body segmentation like rings is the distinctive feature of:
questions.explanation: Body segmentation like rings is the distinctive feature of Annelida.

questions.question: Exoskeleton of cuticle with jointed appendages is the distinctive feature of:
questions.explanation: Exoskeleton of cuticle with jointed appendages is the distinctive feature of Arthropoda.

questions.question: External skeleton of shell usually present is the distinctive feature of:
questions.explanation: External skeleton of shell usually present is the distinctive feature of Mollusca.

questions.question: Water vascular system and radial symmetry is the distinctive feature of:
questions.explanation: Water vascular system and radial symmetry is the distinctive feature of Echinodermata.

questions.question: Worm-like with proboscis, collar and trunk is the distinctive feature of:
questions.explanation: Worm-like body with proboscis, collar and trunk is the distinctive feature of Hemichordata.

questions.question: Notochord, dorsal hollow nerve cord, gill slits with limbs or fins is the distinctive feature of:
questions.explanation: Notochord, dorsal hollow nerve cord, gill slits with limbs or fins is the distinctive feature of Phylum Chordata.

questions.question: Which phyla have segmentation present?
questions.explanation: Segmentation is present in Annelida, Arthropoda and Chordata. Among the options, Annelida and Arthropoda both show segmentation.

questions.question: The circulatory system is absent in which phyla?
questions.explanation: The circulatory system is absent in Porifera, Coelenterata, Ctenophora, Platyhelminthes and Aschelminthes.

questions.question: Respiratory system is present starting from which phylum onwards?
questions.explanation: According to Table 4.2, respiratory system is present from Arthropoda onwards (Arthropoda, Mollusca, Echinodermata, Hemichordata, Chordata). It is absent in Porifera through Annelida.

questions.question: Phylum Porifera has which level of organisation?
questions.explanation: Porifera has cellular level of organisation.

questions.question: Both Coelenterata and Ctenophora have which level of organisation?
questions.explanation: Both Coelenterata (Cnidaria) and Ctenophora have tissue level of organisation.

questions.question: Platyhelminthes has which level of organisation?
questions.explanation: Platyhelminthes has organ and organ system level of organisation.

questions.question: Which phyla have 'Absent' coelom (acoelomate)?
questions.explanation: Porifera, Coelenterata, Ctenophora, and Platyhelminthes are acoelomate (no coelom).

questions.question: Which phylum has 'pseudocoelom' as its body cavity?
questions.explanation: Only Aschelminthes has a pseudocoelom — the body cavity not lined by mesoderm.

questions.question: Which is the correct sequence of increasing complexity of levels of organisation?
questions.explanation: The correct sequence from simple to complex is: Cellular → Tissue → Organ → Organ system.

questions.question: Digestion is incomplete (one opening) in which phyla?
questions.explanation: Digestive system is incomplete in Coelenterata and Ctenophora (single opening). Platyhelminthes also has incomplete digestion.

questions.question: What is the Gnathostomata super class divided into?
questions.explanation: Gnathostomata is divided into super classes Pisces (bear fins) and Tetrapoda (bear limbs).

questions.question: Which classes fall under super class Pisces?
questions.explanation: Super class Pisces includes Class Chondrichthyes and Class Osteichthyes.

questions.question: Which classes fall under super class Tetrapoda?
questions.explanation: Super class Tetrapoda includes Amphibia, Reptilia, Aves and Mammalia.

questions.question: Pennatula (Sea pen) belongs to which phylum?
questions.explanation: Pennatula (Sea pen) is an example of Phylum Coelenterata.

questions.question: Gorgonia is commonly known as:
questions.explanation: Gorgonia is commonly known as sea fan and is an example of Phylum Coelenterata.

questions.question: Ctenoplana belongs to which phylum?
questions.explanation: Ctenoplana is an example of Phylum Ctenophora.

questions.question: Saccoglossus belongs to which phylum?
questions.explanation: Saccoglossus is an example of Phylum Hemichordata.

questions.question: Doliolum belongs to which subphylum of Chordata?
questions.explanation: Doliolum belongs to Subphylum Urochordata (along with Ascidia and Salpa).

questions.question: Which of the following animals has an open circulatory system AND jointed appendages?
questions.explanation: Arthropoda has an open circulatory system and jointed appendages.

questions.question: Which of the following is exclusively marine?
questions.explanation: All members of Phylum Echinodermata are exclusively marine.

questions.question: Which of the following phyla has 'Various' symmetry?
questions.explanation: Porifera has 'Various' symmetry — mostly asymmetrical but can show other types.

questions.question: Among the vertebrate classes, which are poikilothermous (cold-blooded)?
questions.explanation: Fishes (Chondrichthyes and Osteichthyes), amphibians and reptiles are poikilothermous (cold-blooded).

questions.question: Among the vertebrate classes, which are homoiothermous (warm-blooded)?
questions.explanation: Aves (birds) and Mammalia are homoiothermous (warm-blooded) — they can maintain constant body temperature.

questions.question: In which class does the notochord persist throughout adult life?
questions.explanation: In Chondrichthyes, the notochord is persistent throughout life (unlike in vertebrates where it is replaced by vertebral column).

questions.question: Pristis is commonly known as:
questions.explanation: Pristis is commonly known as saw fish, belonging to Class Chondrichthyes.

questions.question: Catla (Katla) and Clarias (Magur) are examples of:
questions.explanation: Catla (Katla) and Clarias (Magur) are freshwater bony fishes (Osteichthyes).

questions.question: Pterophyllum is commonly known as:
questions.explanation: Pterophyllum is commonly known as angel fish, an aquarium bony fish.

questions.question: Hyla is the scientific name for:
questions.explanation: Hyla is the scientific name for tree frog, belonging to Class Amphibia.

questions.question: Calotes is commonly known as:
questions.explanation: Calotes is commonly known as garden lizard, belonging to Class Reptilia.

questions.question: Bangarus is the scientific name for:
questions.explanation: Bangarus is the scientific name for krait, a poisonous snake belonging to Class Reptilia.

questions.question: Hemidactylus is commonly known as:
questions.explanation: Hemidactylus is commonly known as wall lizard, belonging to Class Reptilia.

questions.question: Psittacula is the scientific name for:
questions.explanation: Psittacula is the scientific name for parrot, belonging to Class Aves.

questions.question: Columba is the scientific name for:
questions.explanation: Columba is the scientific name for pigeon, belonging to Class Aves.

questions.question: Struthio is the scientific name for:
questions.explanation: Struthio is the scientific name for ostrich, the largest flightless bird.

questions.question: Delphinus is the scientific name for:
questions.explanation: Delphinus is the scientific name for common dolphin, belonging to Class Mammalia.

questions.question: Equus is the scientific name for:
questions.explanation: Equus is the scientific name for horse, belonging to Class Mammalia.

questions.question: Elephas is the scientific name for:
questions.explanation: Elephas is the scientific name for elephant, belonging to Class Mammalia.

questions.question: Which of the following is an example of a viviparous mammal?
questions.explanation: Macropus (Kangaroo) is viviparous. Ornithorhynchus (Platypus) is the oviparous exception among mammals.
